tagged_story_for_tts:
Hidden preferences.<[silence:600ms]> Machines copying mistakes.<[silence:600ms]>

The term human bias in algorithms describes how computers, trained on human data, accidentally adopt our unfair habits—like a student copying a teacher’s errors without realizing it.<[silence:600ms]>

Algorithms learn from past decisions:<[silence:200ms]> loan approvals, job applications, even prison sentences.<[silence:600ms]> If historical data favors one group (e.g., hiring more men for tech jobs), the algorithm assumes that’s the "correct" pattern.<[silence:600ms]> Example:<[silence:200ms]> In 2018, Amazon scrapped a hiring tool after it downgraded resumes containing "women’s" (e.g., "women’s chess club") because most past hires were men.<[silence:600ms]> The system didn’t invent bias—it mirrored existing imbalances, like a funhouse mirror stretching flaws we already ignore.<[silence:600ms]>

Worse, biases hide in proxies.<[silence:600ms]> An algorithm might reject loans for ZIP codes with lower incomes, indirectly targeting racial minorities without ever using race as a variable.<[silence:600ms]> It’s like banning red shoes to exclude clowns—technically neutral, but the outcome isn’t.<[silence:600ms]>

Fixing it requires audits (e.g., testing if an algorithm treats "Jamal" and "Greg" equally) and diverse training data.<[silence:600ms]> Yet even "fair" algorithms can fail:<[silence:200ms]> A 2019 study found crime-prediction tools flagged Black defendants as higher-risk than white defendants with identical records.<[silence:600ms]>

Three related subjects are data poisoning attacks, ethical artificial intelligence, and how statistics lie.<[silence:600ms]>
clean_story:
Hidden preferences. Machines copying mistakes.

The term human bias in algorithms describes how computers, trained on human data, accidentally adopt our unfair habits—like a student copying a teacher’s errors without realizing it.

Algorithms learn from past decisions: loan approvals, job applications, even prison sentences. If historical data favors one group (e.g., hiring more men for tech jobs), the algorithm assumes that’s the "correct" pattern. Example: In 2018, Amazon scrapped a hiring tool after it downgraded resumes containing "women’s" (e.g., "women’s chess club") because most past hires were men. The system didn’t invent bias—it mirrored existing imbalances, like a funhouse mirror stretching flaws we already ignore.

Worse, biases hide in proxies. An algorithm might reject loans for ZIP codes with lower incomes, indirectly targeting racial minorities without ever using race as a variable. It’s like banning red shoes to exclude clowns—technically neutral, but the outcome isn’t.

Fixing it requires audits (e.g., testing if an algorithm treats "Jamal" and "Greg" equally) and diverse training data. Yet even "fair" algorithms can fail: A 2019 study found crime-prediction tools flagged Black defendants as higher-risk than white defendants with identical records.

Three related subjects are data poisoning attacks, ethical artificial intelligence, and how statistics lie.
